## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  First end-to-end execution of the gauntlet suite via rbw-tP against actual fresh state. The bug class this heat exists to catch — silent first-build assumptions — surfaces here in its remaining form: case-level probes have caught what they can during walkthrough and fixture construction; this pace exposes the cross-section bugs only the full assembled sequence reveals.
  
  **Wrap criterion is green tally.** Not "ran to completion." Not "captured findings." Not "construction complete." This pace exists as a structural guardrail against the recurring agent failure mode where wrap is driven at "I built the thing" rather than "the thing actually works against reality." If the first run surfaces issues, fixes land here (small) or spawn follow-up paces (large) — but this pace stays open until rbw-tP runs green end-to-end.
  
  Intent-only docket. Triage decisions surface at mount time per surprise.
  
  Post-BBABB landing: this pace may run concurrently with BBABA on a different machine when both stations carry distinct BURS_TINCTURE values. Tincture composition makes RBDC-derived names disjoint per station; the remaining collision surface is operator error (same tincture on both stations).
  
  ## Cost
  
  ~1 hour wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt (one throwaway from pristine-lifecycle fixture, one canonical from canonical-establish). Re-runs after inline fixes accumulate cost; budget accordingly.
  
  ## Scope
  
  - Marshal zero per RELEASE.md §2 (signature commit on HEAD)
  - Run rbw-tP, which enforces entry contract and exec's the gauntlet suite tabtarget
  - Capture every surprise — failed assertions, unexpected diagnostics, performance anomalies, missing tabtargets, awkward UX
  - Triage routing per surprise:
    - Tiny inline (one-line bash, message clarity) — fix and re-run within this pace
    - Larger (structural, design call) — slate as follow-up pace, surface to operator before deciding
  - Record into pace closure: wall-clock total, per-phase wall-clock, cloud cost, surprises + dispositions
  
  ## Collision-aware diagnostic
  
  Any GCP "resource already exists" or equivalent name-collision error during the run produces a diagnostic that names BURS_TINCTURE and the colliding term, with a hypothesis pointing at peer-station tincture overlap. Distinguishes tincture-composition collision from transient GCP race.
  
  ## Constraints
  
  - Honor paddock's Release-branch execution contract
  - **Wrap criterion is green tally** — explicit guardrail, restated to defeat drift
  - Re-runs within the pace are first-class; each re-run draws fresh quota
  - Per BBAAN's invest-fatal-on-exists and divest-deletes-production-RBRA: any retry path must respect cult-verb posture; reset between attempts goes through marshal-zero, not partial recovery
  - Per RELEASE.md failure mode contract: mid-qualification failure means start over from marshal-zero, not patch-and-continue
  
  ## Cross-pace dependencies
  
  - gauntlet-suite-assembly landed (rbw-tP and the gauntlet suite tabtarget exist)
  - BBABB landed (BURS_TINCTURE composition; required for the parallel-station property)
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - Restoring RBRAs — gauntlet self-mints all role credentials (BBAAN's invest cult-verb); only Payor OAuth is the operator's prerequisite
  - /rbk-prep-release integration (BBAAH)
  - Multi-day re-run scenarios
  - Cleanup beyond what rbw-tP itself does — operator peeks post-success and runs cleanup tabtargets manually if desired

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  First end-to-end execution of the gauntlet suite via rbw-tP against actual fresh state. The bug class this heat exists to catch — silent first-build assumptions — surfaces here in its remaining form: case-level probes have caught what they can during walkthrough and fixture construction; this pace exposes the cross-section bugs only the full assembled sequence reveals.
  
  **Wrap criterion is green tally.** Not "ran to completion." Not "captured findings." Not "construction complete." This pace exists as a structural guardrail against the recurring agent failure mode where wrap is driven at "I built the thing" rather than "the thing actually works against reality." If the first run surfaces issues, fixes land here (small) or spawn follow-up paces (large) — but this pace stays open until rbw-tP runs green end-to-end.
  
  Intent-only docket. Triage decisions surface at mount time per surprise.
  
  Post-BBABB landing: this pace may run concurrently with BBABA on a different machine when both stations carry distinct BURS_TINCTURE values. Tincture composition makes RBDC-derived names disjoint per station; the remaining collision surface is operator error (same tincture on both stations).
  
  ## Cost
  
  ~1 hour wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt (one throwaway from pristine-lifecycle fixture, one canonical from canonical-establish). Re-runs after inline fixes accumulate cost; budget accordingly.
  
  ## Scope
  
  - Marshal zero per RELEASE.md §2 (signature commit on HEAD)
  - Run rbw-tP, which enforces entry contract and exec's the gauntlet suite tabtarget
  - Capture every surprise — failed assertions, unexpected diagnostics, performance anomalies, missing tabtargets, awkward UX
  - Triage routing per surprise:
    - Tiny inline (one-line bash, message clarity) — fix and re-run within this pace
    - Larger (structural, design call) — slate as follow-up pace, surface to operator before deciding
  - Record into pace closure: wall-clock total, per-phase wall-clock, cloud cost, surprises + dispositions
  
  ## Collision-aware diagnostic
  
  Any GCP "resource already exists" or equivalent name-collision error during the run produces a diagnostic that names BURS_TINCTURE and the colliding term, with a hypothesis pointing at peer-station tincture overlap. Distinguishes tincture-composition collision from transient GCP race.
  
  ## Constraints
  
  - Honor paddock's Release-branch execution contract
  - **Wrap criterion is green tally** — explicit guardrail, restated to defeat drift
  - Re-runs within the pace are first-class; each re-run draws fresh quota
  - Per BBAAN's invest-fatal-on-exists and divest-deletes-production-RBRA: any retry path must respect cult-verb posture; reset between attempts goes through marshal-zero, not partial recovery
  - Per RELEASE.md failure mode contract: mid-qualification failure means start over from marshal-zero, not patch-and-continue
  
  ## Cross-pace dependencies
  
  - gauntlet-suite-assembly landed (rbw-tP and the gauntlet suite tabtarget exist)
  - BBABB landed (BURS_TINCTURE composition; required for the parallel-station property)
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - Restoring RBRAs — gauntlet self-mints all role credentials (BBAAN's invest cult-verb); only Payor OAuth is the operator's prerequisite
  - /rbk-prep-release integration (BBAAH)
  - Multi-day re-run scenarios
  - Cleanup beyond what rbw-tP itself does — operator peeks post-success and runs cleanup tabtargets manually if desired

content.text: The orient showed BBAAX as the next actionable pace, but you asked for BBAAw — let me look that one up directly.

Drafted from ₢A-AAS in ₣A-.

## Character
Future follow-up to the handbook-render fixture landed in ₣A6 (₢A6AAt). Design decision pace — likely requires some discussion of how to represent per-colophon parameterization in Theurge's test fixtures before any code moves.

## Docket
The handbook-render fixture in ₣A6 exercises 15 of the 16 handbook colophons. The one skipped is `rbw-HWdw` (`RBZ_HW_DOCKER_WSL_NATIVE`), which uses the `param1` channel — it requires a WSL-target argument to run, and the fixture's uniform "invoke and check exit 0" pattern does not supply one.

Decide how the handbook-render fixture should handle this (and any future param1-channel handbooks). Candidate directions:

- **Per-colophon argument lookup**: extend the Rust-side handbook colophon registry with a `default_args` slice per entry. Most entries get `&[]`; `rbw-HWdw` gets a known-safe dummy. Keeps the case loop uniform, localizes the exception.
- **Dedicated subsection in the fixture**: a second section listing only param1 handbooks with their dummy values. Preserves the "one case per colophon" symmetry without threading argument tables through the uniform case.
- **Split fixture**: `handbook-render-simple` (no args) + `handbook-render-parameterized` (args explicit). Heavier and probably not worth it for one offender.

This pace picks a direction, implements it, and extends handbook-render coverage to `rbw-HWdw`.

### Prerequisites
- ₢A6AAt (handbook-render-fixture) must be complete — this pace patches a known scope deferral from that work.

### Wrap criterion
- handbook-render fixture exercises all 16 handbook colophons (including `rbw-HWdw`)
- Pattern generalizes to any future param1 handbooks added via `rbz_zipper.sh`
- Test asserts the chosen dummy param1 value(s) produce a rendering exit (not an argument-missing exit)

### Notes
- Known exit 0 is not the only valid verdict here. The test's purpose is to catch display breakage in handbook rendering — the dummy argument must steer toward a rendered output path, not an early-exit-due-to-bad-arg path.
- `rbw-HWdw` is currently the only param1 handbook. If a second one lands before this pace runs, reassess whether an inline table is still the right shape.

content.text:
## ₢BBAAw — extend handbook-render to param1 colophons

**Pace goal in one sentence:** Extend the `handbook-render` fixture to cover the one currently-skipped param1-channel handbook colophon (`rbw-HWdw`) and establish a pattern that generalizes to any future param1 handbooks added via the zipper.

**Heat context:** ₣BB rbk-15-mvp-release-qualification, 49/58 done. (Note: `jjx_orient` flagged ₢BBAAX `rbw-tP-first-run` as the next actionable pace — ₢BBAAw is later in the order. Mounting it now is fine; just confirming you meant to skip ahead.)

**Status:** `rough` — drafted from ₢A-AAS in ₣A-. This is explicitly framed as a **design-decision pace**, not a mechanical one. The docket lists candidates and stops; the mount agent and operator pick the shape.

---

### Decisions to make

**Decision 1 — Architectural shape for param1 colophons in the fixture.** Three candidates the docket offers:

| Option | What it costs | What it buys |
|---|---|---|
| **(A) Per-colophon `default_args`** in the Rust handbook registry | New field on every registry entry (most get `&[]`) | Uniform case loop; exception localized to data |
| **(B) Dedicated subsection** within the fixture for param1 handbooks | Two case-emission paths in the fixture | Preserves one-case-per-colophon symmetry without threading args through the common case |
| **(C) Split into two fixtures** `handbook-render-simple` + `-parameterized` | Two fixture entries, two tabtargets | Strong separation; almost certainly overkill for one offender |

The docket itself flags (C) as probably not worth it. The real choice is (A) vs (B). The tension is **load-bearing complexity vs. extension cost**: if `rbw-HWdw` stays the only param1 handbook indefinitely, (B) is the minimum that works; (A) introduces a registry field that's empty for 15 of 16 entries. If a second param1 handbook is plausible, (A) earns its keep.

**Decision 2 — Dummy argument for `rbw-HWdw`.** The docket's wrap criterion explicitly says: the dummy must steer toward a *rendering* exit path, not an *argument-missing* early exit. The colophon takes a WSL-target argument — we need a known-safe value the handbook will accept and render against, not just any non-empty string. I haven't looked at `rbhw0_cli.sh` yet to see what shape of arg is valid; want me to scout that before you decide?

**Decision 3 (implicit) — Reassessment trigger.** The docket says "If a second one lands before this pace runs, reassess whether an inline table is still the right shape." Worth confirming: are any param1-channel handbooks queued or imminent? That changes the (A)/(B) balance.

---

### What I'd want to verify before committing

- Prereq ₢A6AAt landed cleanly and the handbook-render fixture is in the shape the docket assumes
- Current `rbz_zipper.sh` enumeration of handbook colophons — is `rbw-HWdw` definitely the only param1 entry?
- What `rbhw0_cli.sh` accepts as a WSL-target arg and whether any value produces a deterministic render path

How would you like to proceed — should I scout those three points, or do you already have a direction in mind for (A) vs (B)?

- `rbw-HWdd` DockerDesktop

- `rbw-HWdc` DockerContextDiscipline

All five tabtargets exist in `tt/` and the handbook-render fixture exercises four of them (skipping `rbw-HWdw`).

**The deferred scope is still active.** `rbtdrf_handbook.rs:22-25` carries this comment:
> `rbw-HWdw` (RBZ_HW_DOCKER_WSL_NATIVE) uses the param1 channel and is deferred to ₢A-AAS (handbook-render-param1-coverage) in ₣A- — the fixture's uniform "invoke and check exit 0" pattern cannot supply the required WSL-target argument.

That ₢A-AAS / ₣A- reference is itself a small staleness — the docket header on ₢BBAAw says "Drafted from ₢A-AAS in ₣A-," so the work was transferred into ₣BB. The fixture comment didn't get updated to point at ₢BBAAw. Minor; we'd fix it as part of the pace.

**`rbw-HWdw` is still the only param1 handbook.** Among all `rbw-h*`/`rbw-H*` enrollments in the zipper, it's the lone `param1` entry. Plenty of other groups (regime, image, crucible, foundry) have param1 colophons, but the handbook-render fixture is scoped to handbook+onboarding+guide display tabtargets, and `rbw-HWdw` is the singleton offender within that scope.

---

So the pace is genuine — but your instinct that something feels off is worth chasing. Two things make it feel low-priority:

1. **One-offender problem.** Adding a registry field (option A) or a parameterized subsection (option B) to cover exactly one colophon is heavy. The Load-Bearing test asks: does this complexity earn its keep? Today, no — the value is entirely speculative future-proofing.

2. **The scope-deferral note already documents the gap.** Anyone reading `rbtdrf_handbook.rs` sees explicitly that `rbw-HWdw` is uncovered and why. The "test gap" is socially handled.

A reasonable third path the docket doesn't mention: **drop the pace, keep the comment.** The comment becomes the trigger — if a second param1 handbook ever lands, that's the moment to slate the work, because then (A) or (B) is genuinely load-bearing.

Want me to dig deeper, or does that framing change your read on whether to mount this at all?

set -euo pipefail

ZRBHWDN_SOURCED=1

rbhw_docker_wsl_native() {
  zrbhw_sentinel

  buc_doc_brief "Display native Docker daemon installation in WSL (arg: distro-name)"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  local z_distro="${BUZ_FOLIO:-}"
  test -n "${z_distro}" || buc_die "rbhw_docker_wsl_native: distro-name required"

  buh_section  "Native Docker Daemon in WSL"
  buyy_cmd_yawp "${z_distro}"; local -r z_distro_yelp="${z_buym_yelp}"
  buh_line "Install and run dockerd natively inside WSL distro ${z_distro_yelp}."
  buh_e
  buh_step1    "Enter the Distribution:"
  buh_code     "wsl -d ${z_distro}"
  buh_e
  buh_step1    "Install Docker Engine:"
  buh_code     "sudo apt update"
  buh_code     "sudo apt install -y docker.io"
  buh_code     "sudo systemctl enable --now docker"
  buh_e
  buh_step1    "Grant Docker Access to Users:"
  buh_line     "Add each user that needs Docker access to the docker group:"
  buh_code     "sudo usermod -aG docker USERNAME"
  buh_line     "Replace USERNAME with each fundus user account."
  buh_e
  buh_section  "Verification:"
  buh_code     "docker ps"
  buh_line     "Expect: empty container list (no errors)."

}

# No regime, no OAuth, no IAM.

set -euo pipefail

source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buc_command.sh"

zrbhw_furnish() {
  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env "BURD_CONFIG_DIR       " "Configuration directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env "BURD_TOOLS_DIR        " "Project tools root directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env_done || return 0

  source "${BURD_CONFIG_DIR}/rbbc_constants.sh"  || buc_die "Failed to source rbbc_constants.sh"
  local -r z_rbk_kit_dir="${BURD_TOOLS_DIR}/${RBBC_kit_subdir}"
  local -r z_jjk_kit_dir="${BURD_TOOLS_DIR}/jjk"

  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buym_yelp.sh"                                || buc_die "Failed to source buym_yelp.sh"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buh_handbook.sh"                             || buc_die "Failed to source buh_handbook.sh"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buz_zipper.sh"                               || buc_die "Failed to source buz_zipper.sh"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buwz_zipper.sh"                              || buc_die "Failed to source buwz_zipper.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbz_zipper.sh"                              || buc_die "Failed to source rbz_zipper.sh"
  source "${z_jjk_kit_dir}/jjz_zipper.sh"                              || buc_die "Failed to source jjz_zipper.sh"
  zbuz_kindle
  zbuwz_kindle
  zrbz_kindle
  zjjz_kindle
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbh0/rbhwb_base.sh"                         || buc_die "Failed to source rbhwb_base.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbh0/rbhwht_handbook_top.sh"                || buc_die "Failed to source rbhwht_handbook_top.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbh0/rbhw0_top.sh"                          || buc_die "Failed to source rbhw0_top.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbh0/rbhwdd_docker_desktop.sh"              || buc_die "Failed to source rbhwdd_docker_desktop.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbh0/rbhwdn_docker_wsl_native.sh"           || buc_die "Failed to source rbhwdn_docker_wsl_native.sh"
  source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbh0/rbhwcd_docker_context_discipline.sh"   || buc_die "Failed to source rbhwcd_docker_context_discipline.sh"

  zrbhw_kindle
}

buc_execute rbhw_ "Windows Handbook" zrbhw_furnish "$@"

# eof

set -euo pipefail

test -z "${ZRBHW0_SOURCED:-}" || return 0
ZRBHW0_SOURCED=1

rbhw_top() {
  zrbhw_sentinel

  buc_doc_brief "Display Windows handbook orchestrator — full setup sequence"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  buh_section  "Windows Test Infrastructure Setup"
  buh_line     "Complete setup sequence for running Recipe Bottle tests on a Windows host."
  buyy_cmd_yawp "${ZRBHW_WSL_DISTRO}"; local -r z_wsl_distro_yelp="${z_buym_yelp}"
  buh_line "   Target WSL distro: ${z_wsl_distro_yelp}"
  buyy_cmd_yawp "${ZRBHW_DOCKER_CONTEXT}"; local -r z_docker_context_yelp="${z_buym_yelp}"
  buh_line "   Docker context:    ${z_docker_context_yelp}"
  buh_e
  buh_section  "Phase 1: SSH Reachability (BUK — operator-manual prerequisite)"
  buh_tt       "  1. Jurisdiction handbook walkthrough:  " "${BUWZ_HJ0_TOP}"
  buh_line     "  2. SSH client key generation:          Generate SSH client key per vendor docs (e.g., 'ssh-keygen -t ed25519')"
  buh_e
  buh_section  "Phase 2: Admin Trust + sshd Harden (BUK — jurisdiction)"
  buh_tt       "  3. Caparison-Windows (admin SSH + harden): " "${BUWZ_JP_CAPARISON_WIN}" "" " <investiture>"
  buh_e
  buh_section  "Phase 3: Environments (BUK — generic OS install)"
  buh_line     "  4. WSL distribution setup:             Install WSL and create distribution per vendor docs"
  buh_line     "     Use distribution name: ${z_wsl_distro_yelp}"
  buh_line     "  5. Cygwin installation:                Install Cygwin POSIX userland per vendor docs"
  buh_e
  buh_section  "Phase 4: Workload Provisioning (BUK — jurisdiction)"
  buh_tt       "  6. Garrison-Cygwin (workload):         " "${BUWZ_JP_GARRISON_CYGWIN}" "" " <investiture>"
  buh_tt       "  7. Garrison-WSL (workload):            " "${BUWZ_JP_GARRISON_WSL}" "" " <investiture>"
  buh_e
  buh_section  "Phase 5: User Provisioning (JJK — fundus accounts)"
  buh_line     "  8. Fundus user provisioning runs inside WSL:"
  buh_tt       "     Phase 1 (create users):             " "${JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE1}"
  buh_tt       "     Phase 2 (clone repos):              " "${JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2}"
  buh_e
  buh_section  "Phase 6: Docker (RBK — project-specific)"
  buh_tt       "  9. Docker Desktop install:             " "${RBZ_HW_DOCKER_DESKTOP}"
  buh_tt       " 10. Native dockerd in WSL:              " "${RBZ_HW_DOCKER_WSL_NATIVE}"
  buh_line "     Pass distro name: ${z_wsl_distro_yelp}"
  buh_tt       " 11. Docker context discipline:          " "${RBZ_HW_DOCKER_CONTEXT}"

}

# eof
